CURRENT AFFAIRS.

A correspondent, in the course of a long letter, evi-
dently not intended for publication, complains at not
having seen anything in last week’s LEADER about the
concrete blocks building question. Into the question of
whether concrete blocks are prejudicial to health or
not we do not enter, for we are mnot experts in tlfis
matter. From the industrial point of view, the dis-
placement, of Irish bricks by concrete, made largely of

- foreign cement, is a serious matter. Our corregponc_lent
saysi— It is only when our brick industry is killed
and our tradesmen scattered that the shoddy, unhealthy
nature of the new craze would become known. By that
time probably the present brick machinery would have
largely gone to the scrap heap, and sanity having re-
sumed its sway, no native bricks could be procured

~ and the foreign ones would have a clear field.”

As we have said, we are not in a position to express
an opinion on the health or anti-health pro.pertles' of
oncrete blocks, but two Irish industrial questions arise.

§ we often say, one of, the great mneeds of industrial

Ireland is more production. . Why does not Ireland
produce its own cement? The cement question has been
before the public for a long time, yet, so far as we know,
only one firm make cement in Ireland, and their out-
put, almost needless to say, is incapable of covering the
cement used in Ireland. In 1905 we imported cement
to the value of £215,115, and exported cement to the
amount of £403, so that before this question of cement
blocks was raised, we used over a fifth (f a
million pounds sterling worth of foreign cement. Why
do not some people make an effort to produce this
enormous amount of cement that is used in Ireland, and
which, owing to its not being produced here, has to be
imported? Now on the question of bricks. If these
cement blocks displace Irish-made bricks it will be hard
on the Irish brick industry. In 1905 we imported
bricks to the extent of £16,405, fireclay bricks to the
extent of £17,007, tiles to the extent of £42,469, fire-
clay goods to the extent of £146,389, unclassified
earthenware to the extent of £64,386, and sewer and
drain pipes to the extent of £13,389, whilst our exports
were practically negligible quantities—bricks, for in-
stance, £429. 1In the way of Irish bricks, tiles and
earthenware for Irish consumption, there is a big mar-
gin for extension of business.

One of the big wholesale drapery establishments in
Manchester are issuing invitations, or whatever they
may be called, to all the drapery shops in Ireland re-
specting the autumn fashions, etc. Whether they suc-
ceeded in doing so or not we cannot say, but they made
an effort to have the invitation translated into Irish.
These canny Scots take an objective view of the trade
world. If Irish is on the rise in Ireland, well, then,
as tradesmen, they conclude that it is good tradesman-
ship to flaunt a bit of Irish in attempting to do their
Irish trade.

The Finn Valley Co-operative Agricultural and Dairy
Society, Ltd., Crossroads, Killygordon, Co. Donegal,
want, or recently wanted, a Secretary. Evidently this
Society are on the look out for a cheap Secretary, for
all whom it concerns are informed that ¢ This appoint-
ment would be suitable for army or police pensioners.”

A correspondent writes:—‘‘ Anent a query in your
last issue, ¢ Who are the Nugents of Jamestown Court?
It may be interesting to note that the present occupier
of that residence is a descendant of the famous Geoghe-
gan of Jamestown, whose daring achievement is
chronicled in Mitchell’s ¢ History of Ireland.’ 1In
accordance with the infamous Penal enactment of the
times, this gentleman was required by a fellow-grand
juror, a Mr. Stepney, who, needless to say, was a
Protestant, to surrender to him his team of magnificent
horses which he had been in the habit of driving into
Mullingat, for the usual paltry sum. Whilst seeming
to acquiesce in this arrangement, the valiant Geoghegan,
sooner than comply with this audacious demand, shot his
horses, and coolly informed Stepney of the deed. Strange
to relate, he escaped the consequences of his fearless
action, and until his death continued to drive a team of
oxen to his carriage in lieu of horses.  Castletown
Geoghegan, in Westmeath, derives its title from the
family. The Geoghegans and Nugents claimed affinity
to the saintly foundress of the Presentation Order,
Nano Nagle and Sir Richard Nagle, Bart. Shades of=
our Forefathers! Comment is needless.”” °

There was a fine, windy, old-time ring about the
address which ‘‘we, the members of the Longford
County Council,” presented to Mr. John Redmond.

THE LEADER. 
SEPTEMBER 21, 1907. 
Here is a gem:-". . 
. 
and, in union with the' 
Irish race, we are determined and pledge ourselves to 
devote our liberties, and, if necessary, . our liv_es, , to 
' burst asunder the thorny links that at present bmd us 
in galling slavery to England." 
Who will give us 
Irish a sense of the ridiculous ! Picture a solemn body of 
·County Councillors pledging themselves-not to abstain . 
from intoxicating liquor, but--to devote their "liber-
ties," though in the same sentence they declare that 
they are bound in " galling slavery " by " thorny 
links," whatever they are, to England! And they will 
not only devote their "liberties," but their lives-if 
necessary! 
In the course of an ad'dress from the Local Branch of 
the U. I. L., there was a laughable hash made of a 
hackneyed tag-though, perhaps, it may h.ave been the 
" Freeman " 
that was responsible. 
Anyway, the 
U. I. L. of Longford are reported as declaring in their 
address:-
"Freedom's battle once begun, 
The battle oft is ever won." 
Evidently Mr. Redmond got a very hearty reception 
in Longford. 
He emphasised the over-taxation griev-
ance. 
One great direct method of bringing the over-
taxation question to a head is for certain individuals to 
re~use, on principle, to pay Income Tax and fight the 
question out.. The people set apart by circumstances, 
.it seems to us, for a fight against the Income Tax, are 
people of what is called "independent means." 
We 
have seen, however, that Mr. John Sweetman does not 
propose to defy the Income Tax Collector, and Mr. 
Edward Martyn, so far as we are aware, has not yet 
made any pronouncement, so that we take it he, too, 
will meekly pay up. 
Probably some United Irish 
Leaguers will step into the breach, and, by passively 
resisting the British Income Tax gatherers, raise the 
over-taxation question to a head. Mr. John Redmond, 
in regard to the plank bed, is more robust in his advice 
-and he knows what the plank bed is-than Mr. John 
Sweetman, one of our Vice-Presidents of the "National 
Council." Mr. John Sweetman recently declared : " I 
never recommended anyone to get himself imprisoned." 
At Longford, Mr. John Redmond said:-" Why, what 
is a little imprisonment to an Irish Nationalist? My 
own feeling was that I was never worth my $alt until I 
had slept on a plank bed (cheers). 
Let the young men 
be regardless absolutely of such consequences to them-
selves. 
Let their English masters see that they are in 
earnest, and will stand no nonsense upon this Irish 
question, that we will have to be governed as free-born 
men by laws made by ourselves, as is the case of every 
part of the British Empire except in Ireland. 
Let 
them see that we are determined ; that pending the 
realisation of that hope we will, if legislation is not 
passed, endeavour to use other methods to promote re-
forms (cheers). Let them understand once and for all 
that if they cannot govern Ireland by methods of free-
dom, they won't be able to govern Ireland at all 
(cheers)." Apart from any other considerations, young 
men may see that there is a greater spice of danger in 
politically following John Redmond than in follow~ng 
John Sweetman, for the latter John declared: "I never 
recommended anyone to get himself impri~oned." 
At a recent meeting of the City Technical Com-
mittee in Limerick, a member i_nquired "if the Iris_h 
Language Class had been allowed to drop out of thell' 
syllabus." 
The answer being ''. Yes," Mr. Whelan 
appropriately remarked that: " That did not show a 
very healthy sign of the times, having regard to the 
progress the movement was maki
1ng· outside.''. If ~he 
Committee cannot be congratulated for removm~ Insh 
from the subjects taught, there is apparent!y, _however, 
small ground to blame them for it. Y:, e ,~ead m a :lo~al 
contemporary that Mr. Corn~rton· said: 
The qu·est~on 
had been ,fully discussed pr1qr to the syllabus bemg 
compiled, and the members present cam? to the con-
clusion that it was not worth while .to. contmue the class, 
having regard to the' small attendance last session." 
Another member, Mr. Bourke,' spoke as a member of 
the · class, " which last year dwindled down in atten-
dance until the roll number was only three. 
Sometimes 
there were no students;" and this statement was sup-
plemented by Father Murphy, with the comment: 
" That happened several times in succession." And so, 
though through a different cause, Irish is now' excluded 
from the programme of the Limerick Technical Com-· 
mittee as it is from that of the authorities of the School 
of Commerce in Rathmines. It is a curious situation .. 
The ~ormer body include Irish in their syllabus and 
drop 1t through lack of support. An anti-Irish majority 
of the latter body refuse to put it on at all in defiance 
of the advice of the principal of the school, who, ap-
parently, anticipatec]. no lack of pupils for a11 Irish class 
even in West-British "Wrathmines." 
In the course of the discussion-referring, we pre-
sume, to the local branch of the Gaelic League-Mr. 
Johnson said : " If anyone was desirous of learning the 
language, 
the Central Institute of the • movement, 
Thomas Street, was there for, him. He guaranteed he 
·would be received there with a ' Cead mile failte.' " 
We are glad to note that this speaker s·ubsequently re-
pudiated a statement made by Mr. Hayes, who said: 
" It looked as if the Gaelic League were antagonistic 
to the class there, from the arguments used.'' 
We ·have 
been shouting for the past six or seven years : "Get 
Irish taught," here, there and elsewhere, and it would 
certainly' be the height of inconsistency on the part of 
Gaelic Leaguers to put obstacles in the way of its in-
clusion in a public school curriculum.

At a recent meeting of the Rathkeale Board of 
Guardians it was agreed that Irish manufacture should 
be specified in the advertisements now being issued for 
the usual yearly supplies. 
The specification is evidently 
badly needed if the statement attributed to one of the 
Guardians present be correct. 
Referring to last year's

Guardian, who was anxious to know " how many mem-
bers of the Board are wearing Irish manufacture 1" 
a Mr. Wm. Sheehan remarked: "How can they wear 
Irish manufacture 1;1ntil it is sold in the shops 1"

. The most humorous part of Bishop Elliott's incon-

He declared :-" Our loyalty cannot pre-
vent us from perceiving that in legislating for Ireland

The Union took place more than a hundred

Instead, it would seem as if

Might we suggest that Bishop Elliott

So tli.at we poor,

Un-

What are the Dubiin Nationalist morning and even-
ing Press doing about the Revision in Rathmines 1 
When election times come round they will, no doubt, 
write glowing articles calling on all the '' tried and 
true " in tl:-e constituency to rally to the flag, and all 
that sort of thing • but what are they doing now in their 
capacity as Nationalist " newspapers " in reporting 
•

there regularly in the Nationalist interests tells us that 
the " Nationalist " daily press compare sadly with the 
Tory news agency in this respect; and, apart from the 
injury which their apathy may .cause to ')tl-e national 
voting strength in the district, he suggests that the 
Nat-ionalist papers miss a lot of good "copy" by, their

Here is an. incident unreported in the " Na-
tionalist" Press which occurred on last Saturday in the 
case of a Nationalist claiming and proving his right to 
tlie franchise. 
A young man turned up to claim l:is 
vcte. 
He had done so in last year's' revision under cir-
cumstances that are worth recalling. 
There was appa-
rently nothing of the Sourface in his appearance when 
he modestly claimed his ;vote last year. 
" Are you of 
age¥" said the Revising Barrister to him. 
" I am," 
was the prompt reply. "I mean are you twenty-one1" 
was the subsequent query from the same source, and his 
reply was again in the affirmative. 
Tr.en the artillery 
of the Tory side opened. 
" Wha.t age are you 1" 
queried the Tory Solicitor. 
"Twenty-two," was the 
answer. 
"Didn't you tell the Court you were twenty-
·oue 1" 
" Sure I couldn't be twenty-two if I weren't 
twenty-one," was the crushing retort. 
He got his vote 
last, year without further ado. 
He came up again this 
year smiling and youthful looking, and was introduced 
to the Court by the solicitor for the Nationalists. 
He 
had gained a year in age, and had evidently not lost in 
wit. The introduction of th·e Nationalist solicitor was 
supplemented by the Tory spokesman with the remark, 
" that this was the cheekiest lad in Rathmines," or 
words to that effect. 
The young man referred to in this 
manner naturally demanded an apology. 
But prob-
ably to the relief of his antagonist the argument was 
diYerted by technical questions from the Revising Bar-
rister as to domicile, etc., which were satisfactorily 
a.nswered by th·e claiJnant. Towards the end of the dis-
cussion the Tory advocate plucked up courage for 
another tussle of wit. 
" Where are you studying 1" he 
asked of the would-be voter. 
" At the Royal," was 
the reply. 
" I believe," said the Revising Barrister, 
"Trinity degrees are more expensive." 
"They are 
worth the money," enthusiastically commented Mr. 
Seddal. 
Turning to the latter our young Nationalist 
quietly remarked-" I wouldn't take a Trinity degree 
if I got it for nothing." He got his vote and the best 
of the argument.. 
The annual Aonach of the Gaelic League in London 
will be held on Thursday, Friday and Saturday of next 
week (September 26th, 27th and 28th), in th~ Royal 
Horticultural Society's Hall, Vincent Square, West-
minster. 
The Aonach will be open from 2 o'clock to 
11 o'clock each day. It is promised that the coming 
Aonach will easily beat all its predecessors in size and 
usefulness to Irish industries, as a considerable number 
of the leading Irish firms are exhibiting this year, and 
steps have been taken to open up a London' connection 
for nearly every exhibitor. 
There is to be a big exhibit 
of agricultural produce, etc., organised by Mr. Harper, 
of the Department of Agriculture, which will be one of 
the leading features of this Aonach. 
The organisation 
of the Aonach has placed a considerable strain upon the 
resources of the Gaelic League of London, but the 
League will be repaid if every Irishman and Irish-
. woman in London attends it; and they ought to attend 
it. No less than five plays will be produced; concerts 
and dances. will be held every evening,. Irish pipers will 
be there, and an Irish orchestra will play Irish music 
at frequent intervals.

Omi readers will find in this issue plenty of food for
roflection on the comparative merits of our numerous
Secondary Convent Schools and Colleges in Ireland.
Next week we will have something to say Editorially
about the distinctions gained by some of our schools,
but this week we will ask our readers to carefully read
the announcements made, and they can make their own
comments. There is one aspect of education in Ireland,
or rather out of Ireland, that we would like to refer to
—the habit of sending young people out of the country
for their schooling. It was only a few days ago that
we heard of a case that really surprised us. Of course
we don’t refer to the sending of certain students to
Germany, for instance, for a special course of study
@]lat: can only be followed there, but we can see no
]ust_lﬁcation whatever for exporting young people to
ordinary Secondary Schools in England, when they
have such a variety of excellent schools in Ireland. Even
those who are not in sympathy with Irish Ireland,
might, in the spirit of supporting Irish manufacture,
patronise a pedinin College or Convent School in Ire-
land rather than export their hopefuls to Albion.

artoi:l:a ;:gnba receni; issue, referred to the Irish-made
ads on sale at the Depot of the TIrish Art Com-
panions. The manufacture of Rosary beads is a new
one in Ireland, and we are glad to learn that since its
establishment it has met with a big measure of success.
Up to about a year ago all the beads used in this
country had to be imported. We have n(zt the ﬁgures_; by
us as we write, but it is apparent that tle sum drained
out of Ireland for this item alone must have been very
considerable.  The Irisk: Art Companions are to be
congratulated on their enterprise in looking for a share
of this demand. The beads for which they are the sole
wholesale distributors are manul'acLlu'(;d Ior. them in a
Dublin factory, and already about 15]111'l;y girls are em-
ployed at the industry. These I.rlsh-mélde beads are
manufactured from Irish horn, which passes tLrough a
variety of machinery processes before the finished
articles are evolved.  They are of various sizes and
colours, and our informant, who had an opportunity of
seeing them in the making, was not a little surprised
to learn that the variety of tint was not the resuln of
artificial colouring, but was the natural property of the
Lorn used. Before leaving the factory the beads are
made up into Rosaries, complete in every detail, and

THE DEPARTMENT IN BEING.

IX.—THE AGRICULTURAL STATION AT
ATHENRY, CO. GALWAY.

% THENRY is at present one of the most ‘‘dis-
/L\ turbed * districts in Ireland—we put the word
disturbed in inverted commas as it is only disturbed
within the meaning of British Law in Ireland. We
have often sighed for the coming of the normal time
when every well-disposed Irishman will be on the side of
the law, and that time will not be until we have the
making of the laws. As things run in this country, the
better the man the more disrespect he has for certain
phases of British Law, and, of course, this abnormal
state of affairs breeds abuses. Unfortunately, Athenry
is as stagnant and sleepy as similar places all over Ire-
land ; and its being ‘‘ disturbed >’ and having an army
of R.I.C. men policing it, and special correspondents
from London daily papers prying round looking for
outrageous ‘‘ copy,’”’ stimulates a spirit of unrest, but
nol of activity and industrial and agricultural progress.
The farm labourers employed by the Department were
locking, per deputation to the Vice-President, for 18s.
a week all the year round—an impossible wage in that
part of the country. Others were casting greedy eyes
on three hundred out of the 670 acres under the Depart-
ment station for division amongst themselves, and some
‘“ prime boys,’”’ disdaining the persuasive methods of
deputations, went and spiked a meadow—we saw two
sacks of the collected spikes in a forge—and placed a
great strain on an Irish-made mowing machine. We
arc glad to say Pierce’s Irish mower came well out of
the ordeal. =~ Whatever sympathy one may have for
cattle driving in reason, the spiking of the meadow of
a public and, in a great measure, popular body created
for the purpose of developing the agricultural industry,
is deplorable and silly. After all, the Athenry Station
pays about £1,000 a year in wages to labourers, and,
so far, it is only in its infancy. If we may express an
opinion—of course it isn’t an expert opinion—the De-
partment uneeds all the land it has kept for itself out
of the purchase of over 1,600 acres—probably in time
it will have to look for more.

Athenry was fixed upon as the site for the Connacht
Station because of its splendid position in relation to
railway transit. A glance at a map of Ireland in one
of the railway guides indicates how conveniently the
place is situated. It is, by the way, eminently a non-
political and non-sectarian site, for only a few miles to
the East resides the notorious Lord Ashford, and a few
miles to the South is Mr. Edward Martyn’s castle at
Ardrahan, where that gentleman sometimes takes a holi-
day. Measured by the map, we do not think that the
Department' can be charged with favouring either of
these well-known men more than the other, so that the
‘“Carrion Crows’’ and the ‘ National Council’’ had
better cry quits.

At Athenry, as at Ballyhaise, and Clona-
kilty, there are students or apprentices learning the

. by post 1s. 3d. Mistory of Inh:ﬂ.;, by Mitohel or

theory and practice of high farming. But in this re-
spect things are only in a temporary stage at Athenry.
The dormitory, class hall, and other offices for the
apprentices are only temporary structures pending the
erection of the new building from the design of Mr.
Scott, the well-known architect of Spiddal Church. The
workmen have not yet commenced operations, but in
the_ course of a year or so this building which has been
designed to accommodate about fifty apprentices, will
bve in full working order. There were twelve appren-
tices during last eleven months’ session which ended
on Saturday last; it was the second session of the Sta-
tion’s history. The workmen are engaged at present on
the foundations of some out offices; one of them is to
b(; an extensive piggery; at the moment they have no
pigs at Athenry farm, for there is no accommodation
for them. There are some poultry there in the ordinary
way, but no elaborate poultry arrangement ; that, too, is
to come.

Professor Campbell strayed quite near the borderland
of poetry when we got into the corn land—there are
about one hundred acres under oats this year. A famous
living Irishman wrote of a time when Ireland would be
aglow with waving yellow corn fields, and here was, in
part, his dream realised. A little encouragement on
our part and we are confident we could have led the
Professor in the cornfield into the sentimental mood.
Had he been the editor of a certain paper that com-
bines poetry with instruction on pig mash, we might
have soared into giddy heights on the occasion. But we
stuck tight to the loam, and talked of rotation of crops.
Here, briefly, are Professor Campbell’s views on rota-
tion:—(1) Oats after grass; (2) roots, including
mangels, swedes, turnips, parsnips, carrots, potatoes,
kohl rabbi, kale; (3) oats with grass-seeds and
clover, and (4) hay and clover. By the way,
“the headlands of the fields under roots at Athenry are
not let run fallow, they are used for growing a late
crop of white turnips; in many, if not most, cases, so
far as we know, the headlands are let go waste.

Athenry Station, amongst other things, is a distribut-
ing depot for bulls, stallions, Spanish jack-asses and
rams. The Spanish jacks are given out free to those
who take them, and at the end of the season are sent
back ; we must say that some of the returned Spaniards
that we saw had none of that haughty air associated
with their nation; the poor beasts, they looked as if
they had been treated any way but well whilst they were
on loan. There are about 60 imported Spaniards con-
nected with the Station. ~Rams are also kept there.
About 100 rams are sent out to Connemara; we saw a
flock of about twenty of them at the farm. The rams
are sold to the farmers of Connemara who require
them, at cheap prices. There are 25 to 30 stallions. A
stallion is either sold on the instalment system or placed
in charge of a groom. One-third of the price is wiped
off altogether and the remaining two-thirds are paid in
instalments over a period of five years. We understand
that there are about 100 Scotch bulls imported into Ire-
land every year for breeding purposes. The . big
farmers pick up what is going and the small men in.the
West might be left out of it if the Department did not
go- into the market early and buy up some bulls and
keep them for the smaller farmers at Athenry.

Professor Campbell has a great memory for individual
cattle; he appeared, also, to be able to tell t_he pedigree
of every cow he saw, and there was no getting back to
the village, though night was rapidly falling, :md.M'r.
Coyle, of the Department, was complaining of a nip in
the air, and Mr. Gallagher, of the Department, was
‘warming himself with cigarettes, until he had seen the
last of the herd. Mr. Megaw, the farm manager, and
the Professor were, no doubt, in a heat walking after

A GAELIC UNIVERSITY.

! I “HERE is no field in which projects apparently ex-
travagant may be discussed with so much reason
as that of Gaelic enterprise. While on the one hand
disappointments and failures have been abundant, on
the other there are many things that have become
common-places to-day, that surpassed the wildest fig~
ments of imagination a few years ago. This is why I
venture to set down some thoughts on a Gaelic Univer-
sity, knowing full well that the means are at present
lacking to put such a scheme into force. Careful stu-
dent of the University Question as I have been, nothing
has been more apparent to me as to others than the real
absence of public enthusiasm, both at home and abroad,
for the various schemes before the country. And I
can’t help thinking that not a little of this coldness is
due to the belief that the new-born offspring of a Chief
Secretary’s ingenuity, if it ever came into being, would,
from a national point of view, be only a little less ob-
jectionable than the various existing institutions.
There has often been talk of a *“Sinn Fein’’ Uni-
versity. The ambiguity of the phrase has occasioned
some loose thinking. Father Finlay, as I understand
him, advocated a strong college, assisted by Irish funds,
whose students should take their degrees in the Royal
University. The scheme is an excellent one, and at
present most feasible, but there is nothing really *“ Sinn
Fein >’ about it. A ‘“ Sinn Fein’’ University would
be one whose students contemned the hall-mark of .the
de facto British Government and rested content with
degrees taking their force from public opinion.  Such
a University was the old Catholic University, and its
Lopeless failure is certainly the strongest argument
against modern Sinn Fein proposals (in the sense of
trusting .to public opinion to defy the law) that I am
aware of. 7'he reason why the old Catholic University
failed was because its degrees rested only on public
opinion and had no legal sanction; its ruin was a
triumph for positive law.  The whole force of the
Church and of Irish opinion was unable to render of
value, even in the eyes of Catholics and Irishmen, dis-
tinctions which the King’s Bench would look upon as
waste-paper ; and I have no hesitation in saying that the
same-thing would occur again to-day. The only branch
of the old Catholic University that succeeded was the
Medical School, in which the students were able to place
their qualifications upon a British legal basis. So much
for the force of Irish opinion in educational matters ab
any rate. ¢
On the other hand, in most language revivals the
foundation of a University devoted to the language has
played an important part, and if there bt_z any truth
in Mr. Martyn’s statements that Peo&)le having interest-
ing things to say don’t say them in raelic, it Is in large
part due to the absence of such an institution. Sup-
posing some new ideas, asto the possibility of the attain-
ment of truth in science, for instance, to occur to some
one, what reasonable prospect would there be of his ex-
pressing those ideas in Gaelic. At present the gulf is,
in practice, quite impassable. I believe, then, that a
Gaelic institution, which should take all knowledge for
its province, must be of immense benefit to Gaplic lit‘era_v-
. ture, and would, on the other hafnd', inspire pu}:hc
enthusiasm, in a degree, which all existing and proposed
University foundations, working in English, have quite
failed to do. s L
The difficulties in the way of starting such an institu-
tion, as a full-fledged University, to c}ash with existing
colleges and give worthless Sinn Fein *’ degrees must
‘ be immense A more modest foundation might, how-

_ ever, have a reasonable prospect of success in the not

too distant future. The summer colleges have been one
of the great successes of the Gaelic movement, and what

I suggest is that such a University institution might be
developed out of them. Summer courses are becoming
quite common in English and continental Universities.
This would be something of such a nature. I suppose,
for the moment an income—no doubt at present un-
attainable—of :£2,000 a year, representing a capital of
£50,000. This is, however, much less than was sub-
scribed for the old Catholic University.  The thing
might even be commenced upon a more modest scale.

Universities in general are closed from June to Octo-
ber. This college, worked, let us suppose, at Ballin-
geary, would have a session of four or of five months
from a date in June to one in October. The country
life would be an attraction to professors and to students
ab that season. It would have a staff of twelve first-
class men, who would each lecture two hours a day in
Gaclic on the various University subjects. Some at-
tempt would be made to make these lectures reasonably
interesting to the students; to .treat ancient and modern
authors as human and not as grammatical beings, for
instance. Each course would be divided into ‘‘months,”’
which could be followed separately, and any given pro-
fessor might only take a month’s course at a time.
There would be three grades in the lectures, so that to
complete a full course a student must attend three sum-
mers. The fees would be nominal. Anybody with a
month’s holidays and enough Gaelic to follow the course
could thus attend the college and improve himself prac-
tically free of charge.

The difficulty at first would be to secure competent
professors. A fee of at least £100 for the four or five
months’ course would be necessary. Nor would they all
know Gaelic at first. The services of translators would,
I fear, be necessary. But, by the use of a phonetic
method, a professor could—if he were paid to do it—
acquire in a month or two enough Gaelic to be able to
read correctly the translation of his lecture. He would
soon come to know Gaelic well himself, and the services
of a number of Gaelic-speaking professors would be
obtainable even at the beginning. The college would
give no degrees, but might issue diplomas. The stu-
dents would go there really for instruction. Some of
the money would have to be spent upon housing, at any
rate, if it were to be near Ballingeary. A

But I don’t want to capture the foundation for
Munster. It might equally well be in Connacht. Or
different colleges might have different faculties. If the
Queen’s College, Galway, buildings could be borrowed
or hired during the summer, they would do excellently
for such a purpose, since Irish is spoken up to the gates.
But I only suggest the barest and roughest outline here.
The scheme may seem daring, but in the long run it is
almost essential to the success of the Gaelic movement.
No college working through the medium of English can
ever do the same work. Perhaps, like one of our poets,
I should end by saying that for the present these
thoughts go forth through the ivory gate.

IRISH FLAX AND IRISH LINEN.

ROM a very early period of history, Ireland has
been celebrated for her textile fabrics. Woollens

of fine fabric, it has been proved, were largely exported
from Ireland to Italy, where they were highly prized for
their excellence, so early as before 1357, when the manu-
facture of fine goods was only being introduced into
England by Edward III. Therefore, this art was not
acquired by the Irish from their English masters, but
there is evidence to prove that trade in woollens was in
full vigour, in Ireland, at a still earlier period, when
the very ancient MS. quoted in the ‘ Della Crusca Dic-
tionary ** was written, at which time Irish serge appears
to have been much used by the luxurious ladies of
Florence. England continued to import woollens, both
from Ireland and the Hans towns, down to Elizabeth’s
reign; and Ireland continued to manufacture and ex-
port woollen of various fabrics until Lord Strafford, who
was Lord Lieuenant in the reign of Charles I., pro-
hibited the practice. ~ Woollen goods were imported

{rom Treland into England in 1216, only 46 years after
the first Anglo-Norman invasion. And still further
back, St.. Donatus, an Irishman who became bishop of
Viesole, in Italy, describes Ireland, in 802, as being a
_Juntry rich in garments and clothing.

These manufactures had arisen in earlier and more
peaceful times, and were remnants of that commerce
and those arts derived from REastern ancestors. The
mode_ of manufacturing linen was brought from ecarly
colonists ; Irish serge, which Spencer (‘‘ Fairy Queen,’
book IIT.) puts on a level with silk, was somewhat stuffs
the soft twilled texture of those fine woollen stuffs
which are manufactured and form great part of the
dresses of the upper classes throughout Iran, and even to
the shores of the Bosphorus—most of the ermin-lined
pelisses of the Turks being composed of it. (‘ Transac-
tions.”” of the R. I. Academy, vol. 15, p. 112).

Of course side by side with the manufacture of linen
in Irelaud, and, in fact, forming its basis, was the cul-
tivation of flax. The early colonists who settled in
Ireland—the Firbolgs, Danaans, and Milesians—were
all of Eastern origin, and they brought with them into
the country the art of agriculture, in which the Eastern
pations—Chaldsa, Phenicia, Egypt and Greece—were
most proficient.  The Egyptians worshipped, as gods,
the animals who laboured in agriculture, and the
Greeks withdrew their subjects from war and employed
them solely in cultivation of the soil.  The Greek
writers dealt with agriculture; Hesiod, contemporary
with Homer, wrote a poem on the subject; besides
whom Democritus, Theophrastus, Xenophon, Aristotle,
and other eminent Greek writers, successfully employed
themselves upon the same interesting topic. The Mile-
sians, who held the sovereignty of Ireland for two
thousand years in advance of the Anglo-Norman inva-
sion, are said to have been expert farmers; and, as
there was constant and close intercourse between Ire-
land and the East during the greater part of their time,
it is quite safe to assume that all the best systems of
agriculture known in the latter countries were intro-
duced into Ireland. The rich harvests which waved
over the ancient site of Chaldea and Phenicia, or
adorned the rich and fertile vale of Egypt, were, no
doubt, duplicated in our own dear Western Isle.
Egypt was celebrated for its linen, and hence that
country must be taken as a great flax-growing centre;
and it must have been from it, or from one of the other
countries named, that the crop was first introduced into
Ireland. In the tribal laws, which obtained under the
Milesians in respect of the tenure of land, due atten-
tion was given to the cultivation of flax, and units of
the tribe were bound to promote the growth and manu-
facture of the fibre, to the satisfaction of their chief.
Through all the vicissitudes of the Danish invasions,
and the Anglo-Norman wars of conquest, the cultiva-
tion of flax and the manufacture of linen still con-
tinued in Ireland, as did also the, perhaps, more
important production of wool, and the industry of fine
woollen fabrics.

In Arthur Young’s ‘“ Tour in Ireland,” (1776-1779),
the author narrates that the only manufacture of con-
siderable importance in Ireland was that of linen, and
that same was considered by the Irish, for near a cen-
tury preceding, as the great staple of the kingdom.
Young says that the history of the linen manufacture in
TIreland was very little known, but that it was men-
tioned, conjointly with the manufacture of woollen yarn,
in an Act passed in 1542, the 33rd of Henry VIIL.;
that the above manufacturers were mentioned in Acts
and Proclamations during the reigns of Elizabeth and
of Charles I, and Charles II., ‘“ from whence,’”” he says,
““it evidently appeared that the Kingdom possessed an
export trade in these commodities at those early
periods.”  The woollen manufactures of England,
which were but of mushroom growth compared with
those of Ireland, were, in these reigns, apparently com-

compact forced on Ireland that she should give up her
woollen manufacture so that the latter‘mlght become
the staple of England, while linen remained the staple
of Ireland. Ilow honourably the compact was kept by
England is shown by the Act passed in the 23rd'of

hemp; as well as in several other enactments made at
different times to increase the English linen manufac-
ture so that it might outrival that of Ireland.

Even under all those disadvantages, however, the ex-
port of linen cloth and yarn from Ireland (eighteen-
twentieths of which was to England) increased by leaps
and bounds, from 1700 onwards. In 1710 the exports
reached a total value of £153,389, and in 1771 the
total ran to £2,108,257, this was the highest total of
any year, and in 1777 it fell to £1,492,496. The
average total of 30 years, before 1748, was £417,600,
and of 30 years, after that date, £1,228,148. In aid
of the industry there was a Linen Board established by
the State, and it disbursed a considerable sum each
year in parliamentary bounties, and it also had at com-
mand, for dishursement, the produce of duties from ex-
ports of canvas and sail-cloth, and bounties on import
of flax-seed. The disbursements by the Linen Board
were in aid of spinning schools, flax shops, etc., but the
largest amounts went to manufacturers, bleachers, uten-
sils, buildings, clerks, linen office, and linen and yarn
halls. According to Arthur Young (from whose ‘“ Tour
in Ireland ’’ all these figures are taken), the annual
grants given in this way, during the middle part of the
18th century, would average something like £30,000
a year. Young, whom it may be here remarked, was a
member of the Dublin, York, and Manchester Societies,
was a scientific, English farmer, and his ‘‘ Tour in Ire-
land ’’ is mainly a report of systems of agriculture he
found in the country on the occasion of his visit. It is-
noticeable in his writings that he discounts trade and
manufactures as sources of wealth, and assigns as the
true source of it farming, mining, and, to a certain
extent, fishing. Under the influence of the latter ideas,
he rather disparages methods of flax culture which he
found in Ireland. He says the farmers of the smaller
class, who grew the flax, were, for the most part,
weavers, and that they paid more attention to their
looms than to their crops, very much to the damage of
the latter—particularly the flax. The picture which he:
draws of flax culture in Ireland is not a pleasing one
from the national point of view, and an Irishman might
feel disposed to deny the accuracy of his statements, were:
it not that the ‘ North-West Society’s Magazine,” a.
home publication 50 years subsequent to Young’s book,
is not more complimentary ; and the same must be said
of the allusion to Irish flax culture contained in the Uni-
ted States Commissioner’s Report, published a few years:
back by the U. 8. Government, and which dealt with
systems of flax culture in all the European countries in
which the crop is grown. Reading these reports—old
and new as they are—one is forced to the conclusion
that flax culture in Ireland was not conducted on true
scientific principles, that there was something wanting:
towards the industry during the past 200 years, and

Association are a body of Ulster linen manufacturers
formed with the laudable object of improving Irish flax,
and their idea in the Millisle experiment is to have a
staff of hands constantly employed all the year round,
either retting or scutching, thus making the handling
of flax an industry instead of being one of the operations
of farming. The objects sought by these experiments is
to discover if the Belgian methods—particularly those of
the Courtrai district—are applicable to Ireland, or, if
necessary, what modifications are required to make them
so applicable. Tach year the results have been more
favourable, and the experiments are being conducted
with this year’s crop.

The following particulars in connection with Millisle,
most courteously furnished by a gentleman who is a
member of the Flax Supply Association, will be found
very interesting, as they give details of Continental
methods and the efforts that are being made to intro-
duce them into Ireland. The gentleman writes:—
“In all the so-called ¢blue’ districts in Belgium
(Bruges, Ghent, Malines, Zele, etc.) and also in Holland,
the retting and marketing of the flax is mainly in the
hands of farmers as in Ireland, but in all these places
the seed is saved in Holland mainly for sowing purposes,
in Belgium mainly for crushing.

‘“In Courtrai, however, the handling of flax is quite
different, there the dealers make flax their one business,
they buy the flax on foot; either in their own nieghbour-
hood, or they go over the rest of Belgium, to Holland,
as far as Groningen, and to France, right into Nor-
mandy ; the dried flax straw is brought by train partly
in late Summer, but chiefly in early Spring, usually
with the seed off, when coming a long distance. The
staff of a dealer is, consequently, employed all the year
round, steeping in the Summer and scutching in the
Winter, and the hands become very expert in their
trade.

““ Our object at Millisle is to test the possibility of in-
troducing this system ino Ireland, using vats in which
we can raise the water to the Summer temperature of
the river Lys,* and regulate the flow through them.
The retting we have already proved to be quite satis-
factory, also the saving of the seed; the main point to
be settled by a succession of experiments extending over-
some years is, whether in an average Irish Summer flax
straw can be satisfactorily dried without its receiving an
undue amount of damage, causing much actual loss of’
yield, and, at the same time, increasing the amount of
labour in its subsequent handling.

““We have, I think, during the past five Summers ex-
perienced weather; certainly not finer than the average:
Summer, and yet, each year with the experience gained
previously, we have been able to improve the condition
in which the straw is saved. There has been no attempt.
made hitherto to conduct the operations on a scale which
could pay, but merely to make them large enough to be
a reliable test, working from 25 to 40 acres each year.
Those who have followed the experiments most closely
are the most impressed with the possibilities they dis-
close.””

same way as the latter’s usefulness is restricted, if the

if there is only a limited atten-

unthrashed corn and the grain were in excellent condi-
tion and of fair quality, and I considered one sample,
which rather unfavourably affected my olfactory nerves,
as a downright good specimen of this year’s ‘‘old’’ oats.

The poultry were shown in cages, placed on either side
of a passage adjacent to the old chapel. I was parti-
cularly struck with the cackling of the geese, and the
long legs of the crowing cockerels. As to breeds and
types, I do not presume to offer an opinion, as, except
before me on the table, I am not a judge of poultry.

I dived into the marquee set apart for flowers, and
found myself in the midst of a veritable fairy scene.
Most beautiful flowers all around and equally beautiful
ladies moving amongst them. The exhibits appeared to
be of the highest class, and I must say the same of the
ladies who graced the scene.” The adjudications seemed

Judging by accounts to hand the first day’s proceed-
ings of the Munster Feis were a great success. The
Feis was opened by the Lord Mayor. Canon O’Leary
(an Tdtam feaotan) delivered a rousing TIrish
address, and Dean Shinkwin, P.P., also made a speech
in Irish. Professor Stockley, of Queen’s College, Cork,
read an interesting paper, entitled ‘“ The National Value
of Trish,” and Mr. M. Holland read a paper on ‘‘ Irish
Art.”’ In the evening there was a concert in the City
Hall. The entries for this Munster Feis are the largest
on record, and some of the literary competitions Lave
drawn, we understand, coatributions of great merit. In
the competition for an original Irish play, there are
eight competitors. In view of the high literary stan-
dard ruling at the Munster Feis, the fact that eight
persons have written eight plays by way of competing
for one of the prizes means a great deal. As we have
to settle up for Press on Tuesday, we cannot deal at
any length with the proceedings, but next week we hope
to have a good deal about them.
